summary: Joel Spolsky describes Fog Creek Software's nine-year journey to articulate its mission, inspired by Kathy Sierra's framework of 'help your users be awesome at something.' He recounts how the company started with the goal of being a great workplace for developers in New York City, but eventually realized that everything they did successfully—the blog, Stack Overflow, FogBugz, conferences—had one common thread: helping software developers make better software. This clarity of mission helped explain past failures (CityDesk flopped because it wasn't for developers) and guided future product decisions like adding source control and code review to FogBugz.
key_points: Kathy Sierra's framework—'We help $TYPE_OF_PERSON be awesome at $THING'—is a powerful litmus test for whether a company will create passionate users.
Fog Creek's original mission of being a great workplace was accomplished but made for a terrible elevator pitch and didn't inspire customers.
Every successful Fog Creek initiative (Joel on Software, Stack Overflow, FogBugz, DevDays) shared a common thread: helping developers make better software.
A clear mission statement retroactively explains both successes and failures—CityDesk flopped because it had nothing to do with developers.
Mission clarity makes product roadmap decisions straightforward: if it helps developers make better software, it belongs in FogBugz.
money_quotes: If you can't explain your mission in the form, 'We help $TYPE_OF_PERSON be awesome at $THING,' you are not going to have passionate users.
The tagline was 'building the company where the best software developers want to work.' It was, to say the least, awkward.
Abercrombie and Fitch: building the apparel store where the hottest teenagers will want to work. Who cares? Not the hot teenagers, I'll tell you that.
Everything we've done successfully has one thing in common: It's all about helping software developers be awesome at making software.
I'm not writing to promote our products. You don't have to buy our products to get the benefit of reading about my experience designing them and building them and selling them.
Our focus on helping developers explains why one of our early products, CityDesk, flopped: it had nothing to do with software developers.
It took almost ten years, but I think we finally got the mission for the next ten nailed.
key_insight: A company's mission should be framed around helping a specific type of person become awesome at something, and that clarity retroactively explains past failures and guides future decisions.
